summary: Jeff Atwood argues that we've entered a golden age of x86 gaming, evidenced by Sony's unprecedented mid-generation PS4 upgrade (the Neo) that treats consoles more like PCs with swappable components. He reviews Intel's Skull Canyon NUC as the pinnacle of this trend - the first Intel on-die GPU that can match console gaming performance in a tiny form factor. While the NUC costs significantly more than a console at $970, it offers vastly superior CPU and SSD performance with the added benefit of external GPU upgradeability via Thunderbolt 3. Atwood predicts this configuration represents what console gaming will look like in a decade.
key_points: Sony's PS4 Neo represents a watershed moment where consoles adopt PC-style hardware upgrades mid-generation, signaling the convergence of console and PC gaming
Intel's Iris Pro 580 GPU is the first on-die Intel graphics that legitimately matches current console performance, achieving ~40fps in Battlefield 4 at 1080p medium settings
The Skull Canyon NUC's disk performance (measured in gigabytes per second) and CPU power (4x faster than console CPUs) vastly exceed current consoles despite similar GPU capability
Thunderbolt 3 external GPU support transforms the upgrade equation, making the GPU - the component that most needs upgrading - easily replaceable
Power efficiency favors the NUC dramatically: 15w idle versus 75w for consoles sitting at their dashboards
money_quotes: The golden age of x86 gaming is well upon us.
To release a new, faster model that is perfectly backwards compatible is almost unprecedented in the console world.
Zero games are meaningfully CPU limited today.
On-die GPU performance increases have massively outpaced CPU performance increases for the last 5 years.
If we are in a golden age of x86 gaming, this configuration is its logical endpoint.
I feel like the Xbox One is, overall, a worse gaming experience than the Xbox 360 was. And that's sad.
Did I mention it has skulls on it? Skulls!
key_insight: The convergence of console and PC gaming on x86 architecture, combined with Intel's first console-competitive integrated GPU and external GPU upgradeability, signals a fundamental shift where the upgrade-friendly PC model will inevitably become the future of all gaming.
